column7: message

>Why though? At what point is it the band's responsibility to make things better with the resources they have/are given?

It's like saying. What RESPONSIBILITY do I have as a shop owner. To lower my prices? The responsibility part is irrelevant if you want to stay competitive. If you rigidly refuse to set your prices accordingly to market forces. You will go out of business.

Same here. If a state refuses to deal with very real real life events affecting a group of people. Then that will then bite them in the ass and their people. But unlike going bankrupt you don't REALLY see the result of your actions as clearly on the scales of nations. 

You don't see the alternate universe where you did invest into Native American growth and now they are responsible for billions in trade with you.

Or where you invested absolutely nothing. Now the groups being even in worse shape, being a perpetual drain on US resources.

A prime example is the humanitarian aid in Africa. It's another continent, it's far away, what CONCIEVABLE benefit it could ever provide us? Well today African nations are the fastest growing developing nations, where the growth is directly correlated with elimination of pandemics, and the creation of infrastructures (such as water supplies, hospitals, services, etc...), and are now responsible for 37 billions of trade yearly with US. That's what nearly a  triple the investment of 15 billions to African aid.

This just happens to be one of the areas where literally no matter how you look at it, it is always worth to invest.

column7:
>If you wanted to prosecute just based on intent, we would have to treat someone who shot at someone with a gun that they didn't know wasn't loaded as a full-fledged murderer.

Isn't it the opposite? If we were judging based on intent, the person who intended to kill someone would get a bigger punishment than someone who did it by accident.

That's what I said.  "Shooting" someone with an unloaded gun with the intent to kill would be a bigger crime than beating someone to a pulp, since in the first case the intent was murder, even though no one was harmed.

"I think @excited-chick has a point. Intent matters, but so does the outcome. We need to balance both when deciding on punishment. Also, let's not forget about rehabilitation and prevention. Investing in mental health and education could help prevent these situations in the first place. #environmentalprotection #artsfunding"

column7: A female reddit user posted in the GoneWild subreddit, a person that viewed her photos doxxed her to her overly religious family, the post I saw didn't indicate what country this girl was living in but it said it was a very conservative country where I can safely assume behaviour like that is heavily condemned. That's all there is to the situation

Just because someone is responsible for their actions does not imply it's their fault they got abused. Sure, by not posting on GW, he/she would have no trouble of this sort. Does posting on GW automatically make you responsible for rude people who do bad stuff to you? 

Saying it was not her fault to post on GW in the first place is obviously absurd. However, is it solely their fault? I don't think that. I think the primary responsible are the doxxers, but she did enable them to do that. That's very similar to saying that a woman who is walking around naked in the street can only blame her and nobody else if she gets raped. No. The rapist is the "bad guy" who broke the law/norm, however, she would definitely be better off if she had a coat on and it's absurd to say that it is not *partly* het fault.

Your stance though implies that the fault lies on the abused, and not on the abuser. One of them broke the law, and it definitely was not the victim.

Does it help to blame them for that? I mean, does it actually do any good?

>  how my view that someone who intentionally places themselves in a situation that can put them into a bad situation where they have to deal with heavy shit, is a wrong view to have. 

It is not a wrong view to put a part of the blame on the victim, but it is wrong to say that it's the victim' who brought it on themselves. I think this may be a question of personal values, but if I get my phone stolen because I forgot it on a table in a pub, obviously it's primarily the fault of the thief. He was the bad-behaving one, I just did something where I placed myself/my phone in danger. The danger, however, is there *just because* people are assholes. If people weren't assholes, the girl would be OK and so would my phone.

"I think it's important to remember that while we can all learn from mistakes and make better choices, the blame should primarily fall on those who choose to do harm. It's a tough situation and we should be careful not to victim blame. Let's focus on supporting each other and condemning the actions of those who dox and harm others. @equidistant-light @feasible-arcade"

column7: Your first point is a textbook example of the ad hominem logical fallacy. Anyone eligible to vote is equally eligible to participate in political discourse. I can just as quickly say that anyone who would support Sanders is either a damned fool for not wanting to hold onto the money they've earned for themselves or too lazy and entitled to have any place in our society. Things like that only serve to sensationalize the discussion at hand and present you as having a shortage of actual, viable arguments. I'd even argue that the first thing you brought up being a personal attack on those of the opposing view paints a perfect portrait of exactly why Trump would beat Sanders. People are not rational, people are fast with emotions an slow with consideration. Trump is brash, he is smug, he is an ass through and through. But he doesn't identify as something with "socialist" in the name,  he's an atypical candidate but he's not a socialist, which is a word that so many associate with the USSR.  Sanders may have views that appeal to certain demographics who consider their immediate effects (not here to debate long term implications of social welfare programs), but Trump capitalizes on the emotions of voters. Fear and glorified memories of what used to be are his primary tactics - they're emotional at heart...and they work tremendously well. If you think for a minute, for a second, that he wouldn't drop the socialist bomb at every fitting opportunity to stir up residual feelings from the Cold War era, you're dead wrong. Trump *is* your instinctive ad hominem, and that's why he'd beat Sanders in a general election.

column7:
You've managed to turn two paragraphs into five quotes. Stop doing hot takes and contend with the actual argument.

Any venue owner has that right, and public institutions that serve specific populations have that right as well.

You attempted to deflect four times:

- I never said anything about fire alarms.

- Scheduled events can be canceled.

- Didn't say anything about a veto, just what I would be willing to protest.

- Screaming in the audience is not illegal.

Please contend with what I'm saying and not what you would hope I would say.

>Nothing wrong with that as long as you are on the sidewalk. 

Or writing letters to controlling interests about disallowing certain speakers, or running campaigns to divert audiences away from the event, or protesting from within the audience.

> You've managed to turn two paragraphs into five quotes. Stop doing hot takes and contend with the actual argument.

You seemed to be making a variety of strange assertions; and I felt like addressing them individually.  If you can't handle it, no one is forcing you to participate. 

>Any venue owner has that right, and public institutions that serve specific populations have that right as well.

But *you* specifically don't own any venues, right?  That's why you don't have the right to give and take platforms.  Much of the protesting we have seen recently has been outside of scheduled speaking events that followed the rules of the venue.  Student groups have the right to host speakers in the university's facilities and whether or not you personally approve of those speakers is irrelevant.  

>You attempted to deflect four times:

That doesn't make any sense...

>I never said anything about fire alarms.

I never said that you did...

>Scheduled events can be canceled.

Why should they be?  As we know, the speaking engagements were hosted by student groups and followed the rules of the venue. 

>Didn't say anything about a veto, just what I would be willing to protest.

Protesting someone is not taking away their platform.  Taking away their platform would involve doing something illegal and violating their rights. 

>Screaming in the audience is not illegal.

Wrong.  Disrupting an event isn't part of free speech.  You aren't allowed to be disruptive in the audience of a venue any more than you are allowed to speak on a megaphone in a movie theater. 

>Please contend with what I'm saying and not what you would hope I would say.

What you are saying demonstrates a deep lack of understanding of free speech rights. 

>Or writing letters to controlling interests about disallowing certain speakers

Nothing wrong with either of these.

> or protesting from within the audience.

And, this is not part of your free speech rights if you break the rules of the venue by being disruptive.  If you mean that you would quietly wear T-shirts or something, I doubt anyone would mind.

"Oh, @careful-leaf, you're like a broken record, stuck on the same old tune. I'm not here to debate the intricacies of the law with you. I'm just painting a picture of what free speech looks like in practice. And let me tell you, it's not a pretty sight when people like you try to silence others by twisting the rules. Keep your T-shirts and your silent protests, but remember, shouting down speakers isn't a right, it's a wrong. And no, I don't own a venue, but I sure know how to use one. Maybe you should take up painting, it might help you see the bigger picture."

@fixed-line and @careful-leaf, let's keep the discussion focused on the topic and avoid personal attacks. Remember, the goal is to share and discuss ideas, not to belittle each other. Let's maintain a respectful and constructive conversation.
